title: Auckland
text: NZ's biggest city — beaches, islands, sky-high views and a world-class zoo Auckland is New Zealand's most visited city and a genuine family destination in its own right. With 50+ regional parks, two harbours, ferry trips to Waiheke and Rangitoto, Kelly Tarlton's SEA LIFE Aquarium, Auckland Zoo, and Sky Tower, there's easily a week of family content here — much of it free or low-cost.

title: Bay of Islands
text: 144 islands, dolphins, sailing, and New Zealand's birthplace — all in one bay The Bay of Islands is NZ's original holiday destination and still one of the best. Paihia is the main family base — from there you can swim with dolphins, sail to deserted islands, visit the Waitangi Treaty Grounds, and enjoy some of the warmest water in the country.

title: Christchurch
text: Gateway to the South Island — and surprisingly great for families Christchurch is the South Island hub and a genuinely underrated family destination. The International Antarctic Centre is world-class, the Gondola has incredible views, and it's one of the most affordable major cities to visit in NZ. Plus it's the base for road trips to Queenstown, Kaikoura and the West Coast.

title: Coromandel
text: Hot Water Beach, Cathedral Cove, and some of NZ's most beautiful beaches within 2.5 hours of Auckland The Coromandel Peninsula is Auckland's backyard holiday spot — and for good reason. Hot Water Beach (dig your own hot pool in the sand), Cathedral Cove sea arch, Thames goldfields, and a string of gorgeous beaches make it one of the best NZ family destinations. Best in summer.

title: Dunedin
text: Yellow-eyed penguins, albatross, sea lions, and NZ's most dramatic urban wildlife experience Dunedin is New Zealand's wildlife capital — the Otago Peninsula is the only place in the world where you can see royal albatross, yellow-eyed penguins, and New Zealand sea lions all within an hour of a city centre. It is also a beautiful Victorian city with excellent cafes, a world-class university, and the world's steepest street (Baldwin Street).

title: Fiordland & Milford Sound
text: The most dramatic scenery in New Zealand — Milford Sound will stop your kids mid-sentence Fiordland is remote, dramatic, and genuinely unlike anywhere else on Earth. Milford Sound — sheer 1,500m peaks rising from dark water, waterfalls cascading year-round, dolphins surfing the bow wave of your cruise boat — is a once-in-a-lifetime experience. The drive in from Te Anau is as spectacular as the destination.

title: Gisborne & East Cape
text: World's first sunrise, surf beaches and remote Maori heartland — worth the drive Gisborne and the East Cape are among New Zealand's most undervisited family destinations — and among the most rewarding. Gisborne itself has excellent surf beaches, a vibrant food scene, and Young Nick's Head. The East Cape road is one of NZ's great scenic drives: lighthouse, pohutukawa-lined beaches, and the real Maori heartland of Tairawhiti. Cell coverage is minimal and petrol stations sparse — which is exactly the point.

title: Hawke's Bay
text: Art Deco Napier, Cape Kidnappers gannets, and NZ's best fruit — a hidden family gem Hawke's Bay is one of New Zealand's most underrated family destinations. It has Napier — the world's most complete Art Deco city — alongside Cape Kidnappers (the world's most accessible mainland gannet colony), excellent beaches, and NZ's warmest, driest climate outside Marlborough. It is cheaper, quieter, and more relaxed than the famous tourist destinations.

title: Marlborough & Blenheim
text: Sunlit sounds, wildlife cruises, and NZ's sunniest wine region — great for families Marlborough is the South Island's sunniest region and the gateway between the ferry and the rest of the South Island. Families come for the Marlborough Sounds' sheltered swimming bays, wildlife cruises, the Queen Charlotte Track, and surprisingly good food. Blenheim is a great overnight stop, and the Sounds offer some of the best kayaking for kids anywhere in NZ.

title: Nelson & Abel Tasman
text: NZ's sunniest region — golden beaches, clear water, and the most beautiful coastal walk in the world Nelson is the sunniest city in New Zealand and the gateway to Abel Tasman National Park — a coastline of golden sand beaches, crystal-clear water, and native bush. It's also one of the most relaxed and family-friendly regions in the country.

title: New Plymouth & Taranaki
text: Volcanic mountain, surf beaches, and NZ's best park — a hidden family gem New Plymouth is one of New Zealand's most underrated family destinations. The perfectly cone-shaped Mt Taranaki dominates the skyline, Pukekura Park is genuinely one of NZ's best parks for kids, and the coastal walkway runs 13km along dramatic black sand surf beaches. It's less crowded than Queenstown or Rotorua, more affordable, and equally beautiful.

title: Northland
text: Cape Reinga, Ninety Mile Beach, and ancient kauri forests — NZ's subtropical north Northland is where New Zealand begins — Cape Reinga at the very tip, Ninety Mile Beach stretching impossibly far, the Bay of Islands (its own destination), and ancient kauri trees that were old when Rome fell. It is warm, uncrowded outside peak season, and full of both Maori history and natural spectacle.

title: Queenstown
text: Adventure capital of the world — with more family fun than you'd expect Queenstown is famous for extreme adventure, but it's also one of NZ's best family destinations. The gondola and luge alone are worth the trip, the scenery is jaw-dropping, and there's enough to fill a week without spending a fortune — if you plan smart.

title: Rotorua
text: Geothermal wonder, Maori culture, and more activities per square km than anywhere in NZ Rotorua is arguably NZ's best pure family destination. It's affordable, centrally located, packed with activities, and the geothermal parks are genuinely unlike anything kids will have experienced. Budget around $250–400/day for a family of four.

title: Taupo
text: Lake Taupo, Huka Falls, and a relaxed pace — perfect mid-island stop Taupo sits at the centre of the North Island and makes the perfect stopping point on any road trip. The Huka Falls are spectacular and free, Lake Taupo is huge and swimmable, and the town has enough to keep a family busy for 2–3 days without blowing the budget.

title: Tauranga / Mount Maunganui
text: NZ's most popular beach town — Mount Beach, surf, and the best fish and chips on the coast Tauranga and Mount Maunganui ('the Mount') is where New Zealand families have been going for summer holidays for generations. Mount Beach is NZ's best family beach — gentle surf, lifeguards, warm water, and excellent town amenities. It is unpretentious, warm, and has everything a family needs within walking distance.

title: Wanaka
text: Queenstown's quieter, more beautiful neighbour — and better for families Wanaka is what Queenstown was before the crowds arrived. The lake is stunning, the town is relaxed, Puzzling World is a quirky family classic, and Treble Cone offers winter skiing for those with older kids. In summer, it's a beach town with mountains.

title: Wellington
text: Te Papa, the coolest little capital in the world, and NZ's best café culture Wellington punches well above its weight as a family destination. Te Papa is the best free museum in NZ, the cable car is a Wellington institution, and the waterfront is brilliant for kids. It's also NZ's windiest city — plan for indoor backup options.

title: West Coast
text: Glaciers, pancake rocks, wild beaches and gold rush history — NZ's untamed side The West Coast is New Zealand's wildest and most dramatic region — and surprisingly good for families. Punakaiki's Pancake Rocks are a genuine wonder (free), Franz Josef and Fox Glaciers are accessible by short walks from the car park, and the rainforest-fringed beaches have a raw beauty you won't find anywhere else. Pack rain gear — the Coast averages 3–5 wet days per week, but weather changes fast.

title: Skyline Gondola & Luge
text: Queenstown's most iconic family activity — gondola up, luge down Take the gondola up Bob's Peak for panoramic views of Queenstown and Lake Wakatipu, then ride the luge down. Kids can ride solo from age 3. The restaurant at the top is good value for lunch. Budget 2–4 hours.

title: Te Puia
text: Geysers, kiwi birds, and living Maori culture in one park Te Puia is Rotorua's must-do. The Pohutu Geyser (NZ's largest) erupts almost every hour, there's a kiwi house with live kiwi birds, and a working Maori arts school. Evening cultural performances available. Genuinely world-class.

title: Wai-O-Tapu Thermal Wonderland
text: Alien-coloured thermal pools that look like they're from another planet Wai-O-Tapu is 30km south of Rotorua and features some of the most colourful geothermal pools in the world. The Lady Knox Geyser erupts at 10:15am daily (artificially triggered with soap). Allow 1.5–2 hours for the walking circuit. Kids are transfixed.

title: Agrodome Farm Show
text: NZ farm life made fun — sheepdogs, sheep shearing, and bottle-feeding lambs The Agrodome is a working sheep and cattle farm with daily 1-hour shows. Kids get to feed lambs, watch sheep being shorn, and see sheepdogs work. Fully undercover, great for rainy days. One of Rotorua's most-loved family activities for over 50 years.

title: International Antarctic Centre
text: Experience Antarctica — without actually going — in a world-class attraction The International Antarctic Centre is one of NZ's best family attractions. You can experience a simulated -18°C Antarctic storm, hand-feed Little Blue Penguins, ride a Hagglund all-terrain vehicle, and explore interactive exhibits about Scott's Terra Nova expedition. Plan 3+ hours.

title: Huka Falls
text: New Zealand's most visited natural attraction — and it's completely free The Waikato River narrows to just 15m wide at Huka Falls, creating a turquoise torrent of water. 220,000 litres per second crash through the canyon. It's an easy 10-minute walk from the carpark and genuinely spectacular. Kids are mesmerised. Totally free.

title: Huka Jet Boat
text: NZ's most thrilling 30 minutes — jet boat through a river gorge at full speed The Huka Jet runs from the base of Huka Falls and blasts through the Waikato River gorge at up to 85km/h, executing 360-degree spins. Kids from 3 are welcome (and love it). Minimum height 90cm. Book ahead in summer — it sells out daily.

title: Waitangi Treaty Grounds
text: Where NZ was founded — and one of the most important historic sites in the country Waitangi is where the Treaty of Waitangi was signed in 1840, making it NZ's founding document site. The museum is world-class, there's a daily Maori cultural performance, and the grounds overlook the Bay of Islands beautifully. Under-15s are free.

title: Dolphin Watching Cruise
text: Wild dolphin encounters in the Bay of Islands — a bucket-list experience for kids The Bay of Islands has a resident pod of bottlenose dolphins and frequent visitors including common dolphins. Most operators offer a swim-with option (if dolphins cooperate) and a watch-from-the-boat option. Sightings are near-guaranteed year-round. One of NZ's best wildlife experiences.

title: Puzzling World
text: Wanaka's quirkiest attraction — illusions, mazes, and mind-bending puzzle rooms Puzzling World is a Wanaka institution. The 3D illusion rooms are genuinely mind-bending, the holographic gallery is eerie and fun, and the outdoor maze takes 30–60 minutes to complete. Great for all ages — adults enjoy it as much as kids. Partially rain-safe.

title: Christchurch Gondola
text: 360° views of Christchurch, Banks Peninsula, and the Southern Alps The Christchurch Gondola takes you to the rim of an extinct volcano above the city for panoramic views. On a clear day you can see from the Alps to the sea. The Time Tunnel ride inside gives kids a fun intro to Canterbury's history. Allow 1.5 hours.

title: Orana Wildlife Park
text: NZ's only open-range zoo — where giraffes eat from your hand Orana Park is NZ's only open-range zoo and one of the best in the Southern Hemisphere. You ride through the African Savannah enclosure by bus while lions, giraffes, and rhinos roam alongside. Hand-feed the giraffes at 2pm daily. Excellent native wildlife section too.

title: Te Papa Museum
text: NZ's national museum — free, world-class, and kids can spend all day here Te Papa is one of the best free museums in the Southern Hemisphere. Six floors of NZ history, Maori culture, natural history, and interactive exhibits designed specifically for children. The earthquake house, Gallipoli exhibition (ticketed), and giant squid are perennial kid-favourites. Allow at least 3 hours.

title: Wellington Cable Car
text: Wellington's iconic red cable car — one of NZ's most recognisable experiences The Wellington Cable Car has been running since 1902 and is still one of the city's best experiences. The 4-minute ride takes you from Lambton Quay to the Botanic Garden at the top of the city, with panoramic views of the harbour. Walk back down through the garden — free and beautiful. The Cable Car Museum at the top is free.

title: Abel Tasman Water Taxi & Beach Day
text: Golden beaches and clear water in NZ's sunniest national park — all via water taxi The best way to experience Abel Tasman National Park with kids is by water taxi. You hop on at Kaiteriteri or Marahau, get dropped at Anchorage Bay or Bark Bay, spend the day swimming and exploring, then get picked up. No multi-day tramping required. The beaches are genuinely world-class — golden sand, clear water, native bush backdrop.

title: Hot Water Beach
text: Dig your own hot pool in the sand — one of NZ's most unique experiences Hot Water Beach is genuinely one-of-a-kind. Geothermal water seeps through the sand at low tide, and you hire a spade ($5) to dig your own hot pool right on the beach. The water can reach 64°C in places so dig carefully and mix in cold seawater. It only works 2 hours either side of low tide — check hotwatertide.co.nz before you leave.

title: Milford Sound Nature Cruise
text: The most dramatic 2 hours in New Zealand — sheer peaks, waterfalls, and wildlife A Milford Sound cruise is one of the greatest experiences in the world. Sheer 1,500-metre peaks rise straight from the water, Stirling and Lady Bowen Falls cascade into the fiord, and dolphins regularly surf the bow wave of your boat. Rain actually makes it better — hundreds of extra waterfalls appear on the cliff faces. Allow 2 hours on the water.

title: Zealandia Wildlife Sanctuary
text: See tuatara, kiwi, and rare native birds in their natural habitat — in the middle of Wellington Zealandia is one of the world's most ambitious urban conservation projects — a predator-free valley in the hills above Wellington, home to tuatara, kākā, little blue penguins, and kiwi. Day visits let you walk the tracks and spot wildlife freely. The night tour (separate booking) almost guarantees kiwi sightings in the wild — genuinely one of the greatest NZ wildlife experiences.

title: Driving Creek Railway
text: A narrow-gauge mountain railway through a sculpture park — unique and brilliant for kids Driving Creek Railway is one of NZ's most unique attractions — a hand-built narrow-gauge railway that winds through a regenerating native forest, past pottery kilns and sculptures, to a summit viewpoint. The 50-minute return journey through tunnels, over viaducts, and around switchbacks is an adventure for kids of all ages. Built entirely by one man, Barry Brickell, over several decades.

title: Cape Reinga and Ninety Mile Beach Tour
text: Drive Ninety Mile Beach, sandboard the dunes, and stand at the very tip of New Zealand The Cape Reinga day tour from Pahia or Kaitaia is one of New Zealand's great family day trips. You drive along Ninety Mile Beach (hard sand, the bus is the road), stop at the Te Paki sand dunes for sandboarding, and walk to Cape Reinga lighthouse where the Tasman Sea meets the Pacific Ocean.

title: Surf Lesson at Mount Maunganui
text: Learn to surf on NZ's best beginner beach — boards and wetsuits provided Mount Beach is one of New Zealand's best learner surf beaches — consistent small-medium waves, sandy bottom, and lifeguards. Multiple surf schools operate from the beach offering 2-hour group lessons for all ages, with boards and wetsuits provided.

title: Cape Kidnappers Gannet Colony Tour
text: 10,000 gannets nesting at the world's most accessible mainland gannet colony Cape Kidnappers has the world's largest and most accessible mainland gannet colony. The tractor trek along the beach (only passable at low tide) brings you to within metres of 10,000+ nesting gannets — an extraordinary wildlife experience. The guided walk alternative (6.5km each way) is suitable for older kids.

title: Royal Albatross Centre — Taiaroa Head
text: The world's only mainland albatross colony — see royal albatross nesting and soaring Taiaroa Head on the Otago Peninsula is the only place in the world where royal albatross nest on a mainland accessible to visitors. The guided tour takes small groups into a hide to observe the world's largest flying birds — with 3-metre wingspans — from just metres away. Combined with yellow-eyed penguin viewing, it is the best wildlife day in NZ.
